old_claim.note: Water Quality Ordinances provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Filtration and water-treatment components help prevent emitter clogging in practical drip-irrigation systems.

source_shape.source_locator: Sections describing water-quality problems that lead to emitter clogging and filtration/treatment maintenance for micro-irrigation systems.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source explains that micro-irrigation emitters can clog from particles, biological growth, and chemical precipitates, and discusses filtration and treatment responses.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports filtration as a practical enabling dependency for drip irrigation while giving no support to medieval water-quality ordinances as a technical prerequisite.

ontology_before: Drip irrigation depended on medieval water-quality ordinances, conflating legal water-protection rules with emitter-clogging control.
ontology_after: Drip irrigation depends on filtration and membrane technology for practical water-quality management in emitter systems.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: drip_irrigation no longer directly depends on water_quality_ordinances and now uses filters_membranes_early as the water-quality technology dependency.
would_reject_if: The drip irrigation node were rescoped to legal irrigation governance rather than emitter-and-tubing technology.
A source showed Netafim-style drip irrigation emerged from medieval water-quality ordinances.
A future node for irrigation water regulation reintroduced water_quality_ordinances with a governance-specific scope.
short_reason: Drip irrigation needs filtration and treatment to manage emitter clogging, not medieval urban water-quality ordinances.
